skill.name: nature-paper2ppt
skill.description: Create Chinese academic PPTX decks from scientific papers, preprints, PDFs, abstracts, figure legends, or reading notes. The skill classifies the paper type, builds a Nature-style evidence narrative, selects key figures, writes slide content and speaker notes, and runs deck quality checks.
skill.summary: Turn scientific papers into Chinese Nature-style PPTX decks with figures, notes, and QA.
skill.author: Community contribution, refactored into static/dynamic layers

security_audit.summary: AI review found that the static high-risk weak-crypto, entropy, path-traversal, and backtick findings are mostly false positives from Markdown prose, code spans, and relative documentation paths. The skill has legitimate elevated behavior because it creates local PPTX output, extracts figure assets, and may use permission-gated local Python tooling or optional renderers. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential access, or data exfiltration evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: Permission-Gated Local Tooling and Dependency Use
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs use of a Python-first PPTX and PDF workflow, and allows creating a local virtual environment and installing minimal packages when policy permits. This is legitimate for deck generation, but marketplace users should expect local command execution only after normal tool and permission controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe local tooling, optional package installation, and programmatic PPTX checks. The risk is moderate because the behavior is permission-gated and purpose-aligned, with no malicious endpoint or user-controlled shell construction found.

medium_findings.title: Local File Creation and Figure Asset Extraction
medium_findings.description: The skill creates a project-local output package, including PPTX files, QA reports, extracted figures, and an asset manifest. This filesystem behavior is expected for a paper-to-PPTX skill, but it should run in a workspace where generated files are acceptable.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-writing behavior is clearly documented and scoped to output directories. I found no evidence of arbitrary path writes, hidden persistence, or attempts to overwrite sensitive files.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution. The cited lines are code spans, tool names, layout labels, and output filenames inside documentation, not executable Ruby or shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context shows Markdown documentation and no executable scripts. No dynamic command construction, shell interpolation, or bundled command runner was found.

low_findings.title: Relative Shared Path Is Not Malicious Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal finding points to a documented shared terminology file path, ../_shared/core/terminology-ledger.md. It is a static relative reference used for shared skill content, not a runtime path built from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only traversal-like string is a fixed documentation path to shared content. I found no evidence that this path is user-controlled or used to access sensitive filesystem locations.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Entropy Alerts Are Content False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptographic-algorithm and high-entropy detections occur in ordinary Markdown text about research presentation structure, figures, and design. No hashing, encryption, encoded payload, packed binary, or obfuscated script content was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review showed prose and academic presentation guidance, not cryptographic APIs or binary blobs. The README entropy warning appears caused by normal multilingual Markdown content.

content.value_statement: Researchers often need clear slide decks from dense papers under time pressure. This skill turns papers into Chinese PPTX presentations with evidence-led structure, selected figures, speaker notes, and QA checks.
actual_capabilities: Classifies papers into discovery, methods, resource, clinical, materials, or review presentation arcs.
Builds Chinese slide plans that follow the paper argument rather than the section order.
Selects key figures and tables as evidence for the deck narrative.
Generates a real PPTX deck with concise slide text, captions, and speaker notes.
Creates QA notes covering slide count, assets, text fit, source labels, and remaining limitations.
Uses optional deeper references for layout, figure extraction, and self-review when needed.
limitations: It depends on available local PPTX and PDF tooling for full deck generation.
Scanned PDFs may require OCR or manual source text before accurate extraction.
It should not invent missing results, numbers, figure details, or unsupported claims.
Rendered slide previews are optional when no reliable headless renderer is available.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Journal Club Deck
use_cases.description: Convert one research paper into a concise Chinese presentation with key figures and discussion points.

use_cases.title: Create a Lab Meeting Presentation
use_cases.description: Build a structured deck that explains the paper question, evidence chain, limitations, and follow-up ideas.

use_cases.title: Summarize a Methods Paper
use_cases.description: Turn an algorithm or tool paper into slides covering the problem, workflow, benchmarks, ablations, and reuse boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Paper Deck
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with one PDF or paper link.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 12-slide Chinese PPTX from this paper for journal club. Include key figures, speaker notes, and a QA report.

prompt_templates.title: Focused Group Meeting
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request with a time limit and audience.
prompt_templates.prompt: Make a 15-minute Chinese group meeting deck from this PDF. Focus on the main claim, evidence chain, limitations, and discussion questions.

prompt_templates.title: Methods Paper Presentation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for an algorithm, tool, or benchmark paper.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Chinese PPTX for this methods paper. Emphasize the bottleneck, architecture, evaluation setup, benchmark results, ablations, and reuse boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Template-Constrained Seminar Deck
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with a provided PPTX template and quality constraints.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use my PPTX template to create a 16-slide Chinese seminar deck. Preserve source labels, crop dense figures carefully, and document all QA checks.

output_examples.input: A Nature paper PDF for a 15-minute journal club report.
output: A Chinese PPTX deck with about 12 to 16 slides.
Speaker notes for slides that need oral explanation.
A short QA report listing inserted figures, checks performed, and unresolved limits.

output_examples.input: An abstract, results text, and figure legends from a methods paper.
output: Slides covering method overview, benchmark evidence, ablation results, and limitations.
Clear placeholders where original figures or exact values are unavailable.

output_examples.input: Reading notes for a review article.
output: A Chinese evidence-map deck organized by themes.
A conceptual framework slide and discussion prompts.
Attribution notes for key ideas and source material.

best_practices: Provide the full paper PDF, figure legends, and any required presentation length or audience context.
Review the detected paper type before deck generation so the narrative arc matches the source.
Ask for figure-focused slides when visual evidence is central to the presentation.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to fabricate missing experimental results, statistics, or figure interpretations.
Do not request exhaustive extraction of every figure when only a short deck is needed.
Do not force all papers into the same slide template or section-by-section manuscript order.

faq.question: Does this skill create an actual PPTX file?
faq.answer: Yes. The intended main output is an editable PPTX deck when local tooling is available.

faq.question: What language does it use by default?
faq.answer: It writes simplified Chinese by default and preserves important technical terms in English when precision requires it.

faq.question: Can it handle papers outside biology or medicine?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes arcs for methods, resources, clinical studies, materials, discovery papers, and reviews.

faq.question: How does it choose figures?
faq.answer: It selects figures and tables that support the paper argument, then crops or splits dense visuals when needed.

faq.question: Will it perform OCR on scanned PDFs?
faq.answer: OCR is not the default. It should use OCR only when normal text extraction fails or the PDF is scanned.

faq.question: What safety review result applies to this skill?
faq.answer: The audit found no malicious behavior, but the skill has medium risk because it generates local files and may use permission-gated tooling.
